// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 2 — ESSENTIALS OF SOFTWARE APPLICATIONS FOR BUSINESS

You have used the word "file" your entire life. But what is it?

Chapter 2 — Essentials of Software Appli — When you open Word and type a memo, you are not creating an abstract document. You are creating a particular arrangement
NARRATION

You have used the word "file" your entire life. But what is it? When you open Word and type a memo, you are not creating an abstract document. You are creating a particular arrangement of data: the words you typed, the font you chose, the margin settings, whether a paragraph is indented, which heading style you applied. All of that lives as encoded information inside the file. The file format , .docx, .pdf, .txt , is the language that encoding is written in.

// SLIDE 02 — THE PRODUCTIVITY SUITE IS NOT

THE PRODUCTIVITY SUITE IS NOT ONE TOOL.

When someone says "Microsoft 365" or "Google Workspace," they are naming an ecosystem of applications, not a single prog

The Productivity Suite Is Not One Tool — Microsoft 365 includes Word (documents), Excel (data and calculations), PowerPoint (presentations), Outlook (email and c
NARRATION

When someone says "Microsoft 365" or "Google Workspace," they are naming an ecosystem of applications, not a single program. Each application in the ecosystem has a specific job. The jobs are different. Using the wrong application is like using a calculator to write a memo , technically possible, practically wrong. Microsoft 365 includes Word (documents), Excel (data and calculations), PowerPoint (presentations), Outlook (email and calendar), OneNote (notes), Teams (chat and meetings), SharePoint (team file storage), and OneDrive (personal cloud storage).

// SLIDE 03 — THE DEEP DIFFERENCE: DESKTOP-F

THE DEEP DIFFERENCE: DESKTOP-FIRST VS. CLOUD-FIRST

Here is the thing about Microsoft 365 that explains almost everything else about it: it was built as desktop software fi

The Deep Difference: Desktop-First vs. C — Google Workspace was built the opposite way. There is no installation. You open a browser, navigate to Google Drive, and
NARRATION

Here is the thing about Microsoft 365 that explains almost everything else about it: it was built as desktop software first. You install Word on your computer. Word lives on your computer. When you save a file, it goes to your hard drive. The cloud , OneDrive, SharePoint , was added later. It works. But it was grafted onto a system that was designed, from the beginning, around the idea that one person opens a file, works on it, and saves it back.

// SLIDE 04 — WHERE FILES LIVE: THE STORAGE

WHERE FILES LIVE: THE STORAGE LAYER

—
OneDrive — The primary audience needs onedrive-specific framing.
SharePoint — The primary audience needs sharepoint-specific framing.
Google Drive — — The primary audience needs google drive —-specific framing.
NARRATION

There is a distinction that resolves most of the cloud-storage confusion: the difference between personal storage and shared storage. OneDrive is Microsoft's personal cloud storage. Your files. You control who sees them. A document on your OneDrive is yours until you choose to share it.

// SLIDE 05 — FILE ORGANIZATION IS NOT HOUSE

FILE ORGANIZATION IS NOT HOUSEKEEPING.

—
Example File-Naming Convention With Columns Showing Bad Name — Example File-Naming Convention With Columns Showing Bad N
Good Name — Good Name — good name applied to the chapter example.
And What Information The Good Name Encodes — And What Information The Good Name Encodes — good name applied to the chapt
NARRATION

There is a tendency to treat file organization as administrative overhead , the kind of thing you do when you have nothing more important to do. This is wrong. File organization is the infrastructure that makes collaboration possible. Consider what happens without it. You need the contract from the Henderson project. You search. You find three files named "Henderson" , one in your Downloads folder, one emailed to you three months ago, one on SharePoint with a different version number. You do not know which is current.

// SLIDE 06 — CHOOSING BETWEEN THE TWO ECOSY

CHOOSING BETWEEN THE TWO ECOSYSTEMS.

—
Real-Time Collaboration — Real-Time Collaboration — google workspace applied to the chapter example.
Offline Work — Offline Work — google workspace applied to the chapter example.
Complex Formatting — Complex Formatting — google workspace applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

The question is not "which is better?" The question is "which is better for this particular job?" Choose Google Workspace when the job is collaborative by nature. A team of four people writing a proposal together, iterating over two weeks, in different time zones. A teacher distributing assignments to students who will fill them out on their phones. A startup where everyone uses whatever device they have.

// SLIDE 07 — THE MOMENT THE MACHINERY BECOM

THE MOMENT THE MACHINERY BECOMES VISIBLE.

The first time you really understand this chapter will probably not be while reading it. It will be when something goes

The Moment the Machinery Becomes Visible — You send a contract to a client as a .docx. They open it on a Mac with slightly different fonts. The layout shifts. A ke
NARRATION

The first time you really understand this chapter will probably not be while reading it. It will be when something goes wrong. You send a contract to a client as a .docx. They open it on a Mac with slightly different fonts. The layout shifts. A key figure moves to the next page. The client gets confused. You have to resend as a .pdf.

// SLIDE 08 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

1.→2.→3.→4.→5.
NARRATION

You have three files on your desk: a memo you drafted in Word, a spreadsheet with quarterly numbers, and a signed contract. For each one, name the format you would use to send it to an external recipient and explain the trade-off you are accepting by choosing that format over the alternatives. Tests: ability to match file format to use case based on editability vs. layout stability. Difficulty: low. Open your own computer's Documents folder right now. Pick five files at random.

// SLIDE 09 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

·Ask it to explain how the original Smalltalk environment differs from modern Office or Google Workspace, and what the mo
Ask"Goldberg famously refused to show Apple's team certain parts of Smalltalk during their PARC visit. What did she refuse
Add the framing"Answer as if you're a Xerox PARC researcher in 1979, demoing Smalltalk to a visiting Steve Jobs"
NARRATION

The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Adele Goldberg was working on Smalltalk at Xerox PARC , the language and environment that pioneered the graphical desktop, object-oriented programming, and the integrated development environments every modern productivity tool descends from decades before most people had heard of the office productivity stack. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

// SLIDE 10 — PROMPTS

PROMPTS

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matt

NARRATION

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matters, direct labels on every mark, one primary red emphasis mark, neutral supporting marks, and no unlabeled boxes. The figure should represent: split diagram showing Microsoft's desktop-first architecture (file goes to local disk goes to optional OneDrive sync) vs. Google's cloud-first architecture (browser goes to cloud document goes to multiple simultaneous editors). Use inline CSS, the pinned D3 CDN, responsive ResizeObserver redraw, SVG title/desc accessibility, keyboard-reachable interactive marks, dark-mode CSS variables, and reduced-motion suppression.

// SLIDE 11 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matt

NARRATION

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matters, direct labels on every mark, one primary red emphasis mark, neutral supporting marks, and no unlabeled boxes. The figure should represent: split diagram showing Microsoft's desktop-first architecture (file goes to local disk goes to optional OneDrive sync) vs. Google's cloud-first architecture (browser goes to cloud document goes to multiple simultaneous editors). Use inline CSS, the pinned D3 CDN, responsive ResizeObserver redraw, SVG title/desc accessibility, keyboard-reachable interactive marks, dark-mode CSS variables, and reduced-motion suppression.
